title: Count smaller elements to the right (BIT/Merge sort)
description: Count elements smaller to the right using BIT or merge sort

examples.explanation:
Step 1: Initialize result = [0, 0, 0, 0]
For i=0 (element 5):
  j=1: 2 < 5 → count=1
  j=2: 6 < 5? No
  j=3: 1 < 5 → count=2
  result[0]=2
For i=1 (element 2):
  j=2: 6 < 2? No
  j=3: 1 < 2 → count=1
  result[1]=1
For i=2 (element 6):
  j=3: 1 < 6 → count=1
  result[2]=1
For i=3 (element 1):
  No elements to right → count=0
  result[3]=0
Final result: [2, 1, 1, 0]

Step 1: Initialize result = [0, 0, 0, 0]
For i=0 (element 1):
  j=1: 2 < 1? No
  j=2: 3 < 1? No
  j=3: 4 < 1? No
  result[0]=0
For i=1 (element 2):
  j=2: 3 < 2? No
  j=3: 4 < 2? No
  result[1]=0
For i=2 (element 3):
  j=3: 4 < 3? No
  result[2]=0
For i=3 (element 4):
  No elements to right → result[3]=0
Final result: [0, 0, 0, 0]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each element, iterate through all elements to its right and count how many are smaller. This is the most straightforward method but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Smaller Elements to the Right Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n²) brute force solution for counting smaller elements to the right of each element in an array.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach solves the problem by directly implementing the definition: for each element in the array, we need to count how many elements to its right are smaller. This is achieved through nested loops. The outer loop iterates through each element from left to right, while the inner loop scans all elements to the right of the current element. For each comparison where the right element is smaller, we increment a counter. The intuition is straightforward: we're literally checking every possible pair (i, j) where i < j and nums[i] > nums[j]. The algorithm has O(n²) time complexity because for n elements, we perform approximately n(n-1)/2 comparisons in the worst case. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the result array (or O(1) if we modify the input array in-place, though typically we return a new array). This approach connects directly to the problem statement—it's the most literal translation of the requirements into code. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors in loop boundaries (should be i from 0 to n-1, j from i+1 to n-1), 2) Forgetting to initialize the result array with zeros, 3) Incorrectly counting equal elements (should only count strictly smaller elements), 4) Modifying the input array when not allowed. The real code pattern involves two nested for loops with a conditional check and accumulation.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize an empty list 'result' of size n with all zeros
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
   a. Initialize count = 0
   b. For j from i+1 to n-1:
      i. If nums[j] < nums[i], increment count
   c. Set result[i] = count
3. Return result

Time complexity: O(n²) - nested loops over n elements
Space complexity: O(n) for the result array (or O(1) if we modify input)

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize result = [0, 0, 0, 0]
For i=0 (element 5):
  j=1: 2 < 5 → count=1
  j=2: 6 < 5? No
  j=3: 1 < 5 → count=2
  result[0]=2
For i=1 (element 2):
  j=2: 6 < 2? No
  j=3: 1 < 2 → count=1
  result[1]=1
For i=2 (element 6):
  j=3: 1 < 6 → count=1
  result[2]=1
For i=3 (element 1):
  No elements to right → count=0
  result[3]=0
Final result: [2, 1, 1, 0]

Step 1: Initialize result = [0, 0, 0, 0]
For i=0 (element 1):
  j=1: 2 < 1? No
  j=2: 3 < 1? No
  j=3: 4 < 1? No
  result[0]=0
For i=1 (element 2):
  j=2: 3 < 2? No
  j=3: 4 < 2? No
  result[1]=0
For i=2 (element 3):
  j=3: 4 < 3? No
  result[2]=0
For i=3 (element 4):
  No elements to right → result[3]=0
Final result: [0, 0, 0, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counting Smaller Elements for a Single Position
ladders.desc: Implement the core counting logic for a single element: count how many elements to its right are smaller.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on solving the problem for just one element at position i. The task is: given an array and an index i, count how many elements from positions i+1 to n-1 are strictly smaller than the element at position i. The intuition is that we need to examine all elements to the right and compare each one with our target element. This is essentially a linear scan through part of the array. The algorithm is simple: initialize a counter to 0, then iterate through all indices j from i+1 to the end of the array. For each j, if array[j] < array[i], increment the counter. Finally, return the counter. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case (when i=0, we scan n-1 elements), but on average O(n/2). Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This sub-routine connects to the full problem (L0) because the full solution simply applies this counting logic to every element in the array. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the inner loop at i instead of i+1 (would compare element with itself), 2) Using ≤ instead of < (would count equal elements, which is incorrect per problem statement), 3) Not handling empty arrays or single-element arrays correctly. The real code pattern is a single for loop with a conditional increment.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Function count_smaller_right(nums, i):
2.   count = 0
3.   For j from i+1 to len(nums)-1:
4.     If nums[j] < nums[i]:
5.       count += 1
6.   Return count

Time complexity: O(n) where n is array length
Space complexity: O(1)

examples.trace:
Target element: nums[0]=5
Initialize count=0
j=1: nums[1]=2, 2<5 → count=1
j=2: nums[2]=6, 6<5? No
j=3: nums[3]=1, 1<5 → count=2
Return count=2

Target element: nums[2]=3
Initialize count=0
j=3: nums[3]=4, 4<3? No
Return count=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Nested Loop Comparison Pattern
ladders.desc: Implement the pattern of comparing each element with all elements to its right using nested loops.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the fundamental algorithmic pattern of using nested loops to compare each element with all subsequent elements. The intuition is that to examine all ordered pairs (i, j) where i < j, we need two loops: an outer loop that controls the first element i, and an inner loop that controls the second element j which must come after i. This pattern appears in many brute force solutions for pairwise comparison problems. The key insight is that j always starts at i+1, ensuring we only look at elements to the right. The algorithm structure is: for i from 0 to n-1, then for j from i+1 to n-1, perform some comparison operation. Time complexity is O(n²) because the inner loop runs approximately n times for each i, with decreasing length each iteration. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop structure itself. This connects to L1 (key sub-routine) because L1 implements the inner loop body for a fixed i, while this level shows how to apply that inner loop to every i. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting j at 0 instead of i+1 (would compare each pair twice and compare elements with themselves), 2) Using wrong loop boundaries (off-by-one errors), 3) Not understanding that the inner loop gets shorter as i increases. The real code pattern is classic nested for loops with i as outer index and j as inner index starting from i+1.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. n = len(nums)
2. For i from 0 to n-1:
3.   For j from i+1 to n-1:
4.     # Compare nums[i] and nums[j] here

Time complexity: O(n²)
Space complexity: O(1)

examples.trace:
n=4
i=0: j=1 (compare 5,2), j=2 (compare 5,6), j=3 (compare 5,1)
i=1: j=2 (compare 2,6), j=3 (compare 2,1)
i=2: j=3 (compare 6,1)
i=3: no j values (inner loop doesn't execute)
Total comparisons: 3+2+1=6

n=3
i=0: j=1 (compare 1,2), j=2 (compare 1,3)
i=1: j=2 (compare 2,3)
i=2: no j values
Total comparisons: 2+1=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse an array while tracking both current position and positions to the right.
ladders.explanation:
This building block teaches how to navigate through an array while maintaining awareness of both the current element's position and how to access elements that come after it. The intuition is that arrays are indexed sequentially, and we need to understand how to reference elements relative to our current position. The core concept is using index variables: one for the current position (i), and another for positions to the right (j). The algorithm involves: 1) Looping through all indices i from 0 to n-1, 2) For each i, looping through indices j from i+1 to n-1. This requires understanding array indexing, loop boundaries, and how indices relate to element positions. Time complexity for traversing all i and j pairs is O(n²), but the focus here is on the indexing pattern rather than efficiency. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 (core logic) because L2 uses this indexing pattern to implement the nested loop comparison. Common mistakes include: 1) Confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, 2) Not understanding that j starts at i+1 (not i), 3) Using wrong loop termination conditions (should be < n not ≤ n for 0-based indexing), 4) Trying to access array elements with invalid indices. The real code pattern involves for loops with range() functions or while loops with index variables.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. n = len(arr)
2. For i in range(0, n):
3.   current = arr[i]
4.   For j in range(i+1, n):
5.     right_element = arr[j]
6.     # Do something with current and right_element

Time complexity: O(n²) for full traversal
Space complexity: O(1)

examples.trace:
n=4
i=0: current=10, j=1 (right=20), j=2 (right=30), j=3 (right=40)
i=1: current=20, j=2 (right=30), j=3 (right=40)
i=2: current=30, j=3 (right=40)
i=3: current=40, no j values
Total index pairs: (0,1), (0,2), (0,3), (1,2), (1,3), (2,3)

n=2
i=0: current=5, j=1 (right=1)
i=1: current=1, no j values
Total index pairs: (0,1)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison in Arrays
ladders.desc: Compare two elements in an array and determine their relative order (less than, equal, greater than).
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation focuses on the fundamental act of comparing two elements from an array. The intuition is simple: given two values, we need to determine their relationship—specifically whether one is strictly less than the other. This is a core operation in many algorithms, especially sorting and searching. The algorithm is straightforward: access the two elements using their indices, then use comparison operators (<, >, ==) to determine their relationship. For counting smaller elements, we specifically need the 'less than' comparison. Time complexity is O(1) for a single comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 (building block) because L3 shows how to access elements at different indices, and this level shows what to do with those elements once accessed. Common mistakes include: 1) Comparing indices instead of array values (e.g., i < j instead of arr[i] < arr[j]), 2) Using wrong comparison operator (≤ instead of < for strictly smaller), 3) Not handling different data types correctly (integers, floats, strings), 4) Forgetting that array indices start at 0. The real code pattern is a simple if statement with a comparison operator.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Given array arr and two indices i and j
2. value1 = arr[i]
3. value2 = arr[j]
4. If value1 > value2: # value2 is smaller than value1
5.   # Do something
6. Or if we want specifically: if value2 < value1:

Time complexity: O(1)
Space complexity: O(1)

examples.trace:
value1 = arr[0] = 5
value2 = arr[1] = 2
Check: 2 < 5? Yes → arr[1] is smaller than arr[0]

value1 = arr[1] = 2
value2 = arr[2] = 3
Check: 3 < 2? No → arr[2] is NOT smaller than arr[1]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: Understand how to access individual elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept covers the most basic operation with arrays: accessing elements by their position (index). The intuition is that arrays store elements in sequential memory locations, and each element has a numerical address called an index. In most programming languages, array indices start at 0, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. The algorithm is simple: to access an element, use the syntax array[index]. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20, and arr[2] gives 30. Time complexity for element access is O(1) since arrays provide direct access via memory addresses. Space complexity is O(1) for the access operation itself. This connects to L4 (basic operation) because L4 requires accessing two elements before comparing them. Common mistakes include: 1) Using 1-based indexing (thinking arr[1] is the first element), 2) Accessing indices outside the array bounds (e.g., arr[3] for a 3-element array—maximum valid index is 2), 3) Confusing the index with the value stored at that index, 4) Not understanding that indices must be integers. The real code pattern is simply using square brackets with an index inside.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Declare an array: arr = [value0, value1, value2, ...]
2. Access element at index i: element = arr[i]
3. Valid indices range from 0 to len(arr)-1

Time complexity: O(1) for access
Space complexity: O(1)

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements: index 0=5, index 1=2, index 2=6, index 3=1
Accessing arr[2] returns the element at position 2, which is 6

Array has 3 elements: index 0=10, index 1=20, index 2=30
Accessing arr[0] returns the first element, which is 10

approaches.name: Binary Indexed Tree O(n log m)
approaches.summary: Use a Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) to maintain frequency counts of values. Process elements from right to left, querying for counts of smaller values and updating the tree. Requires coordinate compression for large value ranges.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Smaller Elements to the Right using Binary Indexed Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BIT with coordinate compression to count elements smaller to the right
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting for each element in an array how many elements to its right are smaller. The Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) approach provides an efficient O(n log m) solution where m is the number of unique values after compression. The intuition is to process the array from right to left, maintaining a frequency count of values seen so far in a BIT. For each element, we query the BIT for the count of values smaller than the current element (which represents elements to the right that are smaller), then update the BIT with the current element's frequency. Since values can be large or negative, coordinate compression maps them to indices 1..m. The algorithm: 1) Create a sorted list of unique values for compression. 2) Initialize BIT of size m+1 (1-indexed). 3) Iterate from right to left: a) Get compressed index of current value. b) Query BIT for sum up to index-1 (count of smaller values). c) Update BIT at index by 1. 4) Store results in reverse order. Time: O(n log m) for n elements with log m BIT operations. Space: O(m) for BIT and compression mapping. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into a complete solution. Common mistakes: Forgetting 1-indexing in BIT, incorrect compression mapping, or off-by-one errors in query ranges. Real code pattern: def countSmaller(nums): unique = sorted(set(nums)); rank = {v:i+1 for i,v in enumerate(unique)}; bit = [0]*(len(unique)+1); res = []; for num in reversed(nums): idx = rank[num]; res.append(query(bit, idx-1)); update(bit, idx, 1); return res[::-1]

examples.trace: Step 1: Unique values sorted: [1,2,5,6], rank: {1:1,2:2,5:3,6:4}. BIT size: 5. Initialize BIT=[0,0,0,0,0]. Process right to left: Element 1: idx=1, query(bit,0)=0, res=[0], update bit at idx=1: BIT=[0,1,1,0,1]. Element 6: idx=4, query(bit,3)=bit sum up to 3: BIT[3]=0 + BIT[2]=1 =1, res=[1,0], update bit at idx=4: BIT=[0,1,1,0,2]. Element 2: idx=2, query(bit,1)=BIT[1]=1, res=[1,1,0], update bit at idx=2: BIT=[0,1,2,2,3]. Element 5: idx=3, query(bit,2)=BIT[2]=2, res=[2,1,1,0], update bit at idx=3: BIT=[0,1,2,3,3]. Reverse res: [2,1,1,0].

Step 1: Unique values sorted: [-1], rank: {-1:1}. BIT size: 2. Initialize BIT=[0,0]. Process right to left: Element -1: idx=1, query(bit,0)=0, res=[0], update bit at idx=1: BIT=[0,1]. Element -1: idx=1, query(bit,0)=0, res=[0,0], update bit at idx=1: BIT=[0,2]. Reverse res: [0,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Coordinate Compression for BIT
ladders.desc: Map array values to compressed indices for efficient BIT operations
ladders.explanation: Coordinate compression is essential when array values have large ranges or are negative, as BIT requires indices starting from 1. The intuition is to assign each unique value a rank based on its sorted order, reducing the value domain to indices 1..m where m is the number of unique values. This allows BIT to operate in O(log m) time regardless of original value magnitudes. Algorithm: 1) Create a sorted list of unique values from the input array. 2) Create a mapping dictionary from each value to its index+1 (1-indexed). 3) For each element in the array, replace it with its compressed index using the mapping. Time: O(n log n) for sorting unique values. Space: O(m) for the mapping. Connection: This feeds into L0 by providing compressed indices for BIT queries and updates. Common mistakes: Not handling duplicates correctly (use set for uniqueness), forgetting 1-indexing, or incorrect sorting order. Real code pattern: def compress(arr): unique = sorted(set(arr)); rank = {v:i+1 for i,v in enumerate(unique)}; return [rank[x] for x in arr], rank. Example: arr=[5,2,6,1] -> unique=[1,2,5,6], rank={1:1,2:2,5:3,6:4}, compressed=[3,2,4,1].

examples.trace: Step 1: Get unique values: set([5,2,6,1]) = {1,2,5,6}. Step 2: Sort unique: [1,2,5,6]. Step 3: Create rank mapping: index 0-> value 1 -> rank 1, index 1-> value 2 -> rank 2, index 2-> value 5 -> rank 3, index 3-> value 6 -> rank 4. Step 4: Compress each element: 5 -> rank 3, 2 -> rank 2, 6 -> rank 4, 1 -> rank 1. Result: [3,2,4,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — BIT Query and Update for Frequency Counts
ladders.desc: Use BIT to query prefix sums and update frequencies for counting smaller elements
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves using a Binary Indexed Tree to maintain a frequency array where each index represents a compressed value, and the BIT allows efficient prefix sum queries and point updates. The intuition is that by processing from right to left, the BIT stores frequencies of values seen so far (to the right). For each current element with compressed index idx, querying the prefix sum up to idx-1 gives the count of smaller values encountered. Then, updating the BIT at idx increments the frequency of the current value. BIT operations: query(i) returns sum of frequencies from 1 to i in O(log n) time by traversing i while i>0: sum += bit[i]; i -= i & -i. update(i, delta) adds delta to frequency at i and propagates to ancestors in O(log n) time: while i <= n: bit[i] += delta; i += i & -i. Time: O(log n) per operation. Space: O(n) for BIT. Connection: This is used in L1 and L0 to implement the counting mechanism. Common mistakes: Using 0-indexing (BIT must be 1-indexed), incorrect query range (idx-1 for smaller values), or not initializing BIT properly. Real code pattern: def query(bit, i): s=0; while i>0: s+=bit[i]; i-=i&-i; return s. def update(bit, i, delta): while i<len(bit): bit[i]+=delta; i+=i&-i.

examples.input: BIT=[0,0,0,0,0], operations: update(2,1), query(3), update(3,1), query(4)
examples.output: After update(2,1): BIT=[0,0,1,1,1]; query(3)=1; after update(3,1): BIT=[0,0,1,2,3]; query(4)=3

examples.input: BIT=[0,0,0,0], operations: update(1,2), query(2), update(4,1), query(4)
examples.output: After update(1,2): BIT=[0,2,2,2]; query(2)=2; after update(4,1): BIT=[0,2,2,3]; query(4)=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Indexed Tree (Fenwick Tree) Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement BIT data structure for efficient prefix sum queries and point updates
ladders.explanation: A Binary Indexed Tree (BIT) is a data structure that supports two operations on an array: prefix sum query (sum of first i elements) and point update (add a value to a single element), both in O(log n) time. The intuition is to store cumulative sums in a tree-like structure where each index i is responsible for a range of indices based on the lowest set bit (i & -i). BIT is 1-indexed: bit[1] covers index 1, bit[2] covers 1-2, bit[3] covers 3, bit[4] covers 1-4, etc. Implementation: Initialize bit array of size n+1 with zeros. Query(i): sum = 0; while i > 0: sum += bit[i]; i -= i & -i; return sum. Update(i, delta): while i <= n: bit[i] += delta; i += i & -i. Time: O(log n) per operation. Space: O(n). Connection: This is the foundation for L2's frequency counting. Common mistakes: Using 0-indexing, incorrect loop conditions, or misunderstanding the range responsibilities. Real code pattern: class BIT: def __init__(self, n): self.n=n; self.bit=[0]*(n+1). def query(self, i): s=0; while i>0: s+=self.bit[i]; i-=i&-i; return s. def update(self, i, delta): while i<=self.n: self.bit[i]+=delta; i+=i&-i.

examples.input: BIT size 4, operations: update(1,5), update(3,2), query(2), query(4)
BIT size 5, operations: update(2,3), update(5,1), query(3), query(5)

testCases.input: BIT size 2, operations: update(2,5), query(1), query(2)
BIT size 6, operations: update(3,4), update(6,2), query(6)
BIT size 4, operations: update(2,1), update(3,1), update(4,1), query(4)
BIT size 3, operations: update(1,2), update(2,3), query(2)
BIT size 8, operations: update(5,2), update(8,3), query(8)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Prefix Sum Query on an Array
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of elements from the start up to a given index
ladders.explanation: A prefix sum is the cumulative sum of elements in an array from the first element up to a specific index. For an array arr of size n, the prefix sum up to index i (0-indexed) is sum(arr[0] + arr[1] + ... + arr[i]). This is a fundamental operation used in many algorithms, including BIT which optimizes it. The intuition is to accumulate sums progressively. Algorithm: Initialize sum=0. Iterate from index 0 to i: sum += arr[j]. Return sum. Time: O(i) linear time. Space: O(1). Connection: This is the naive version that BIT improves upon in L3. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors with indices, or misunderstanding 0-indexing vs 1-indexing. Real code pattern: def prefix_sum(arr, i): s=0; for j in range(i+1): s+=arr[j]; return s. Example: arr=[2,3,1,4], prefix_sum(arr,2) = 2+3+1=6.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: j=0: sum+=arr[0]=1 -> sum=1. Step 3: j=1: sum+=arr[1]=2 -> sum=3. Step 4: j=2: sum+=arr[2]=3 -> sum=6. Result: 6.
Step 1: sum=0. Step 2: j=0: sum+=10 -> 10. Step 3: j=1: sum+=-5 -> 5. Step 4: j=2: sum+=3 -> 8. Step 5: j=3: sum+=7 -> 15. Result: 15.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Traversal and Element Access
ladders.desc: Basic operations: iterate through an array and access elements by index
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves understanding how to work with arrays: accessing elements by their index and traversing from start to end or in reverse. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, indexed starting from 0. Key operations: 1) Access: arr[i] retrieves the element at index i. 2) Traversal forward: for i in range(len(arr)): process arr[i]. 3) Traversal backward: for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): process arr[i]. The intuition is that arrays allow random access in O(1) time, making them efficient for many algorithms. Time: O(n) for full traversal. Space: O(1) for traversal without extra storage. Connection: This is prerequisite for L4's prefix sums and higher levels. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing arr[len(arr)]), or incorrect loop ranges for reverse traversal. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]) prints 1,2,3. For reverse: for i in range(len(arr)-1, -1, -1): print(arr[i]) prints 3,2,1.

examples.input: arr=[5,10,15], traverse forward and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: len(arr)=3. Step 2: i=0: access arr[0]=5, print 5. Step 3: i=1: access arr[1]=10, print 10. Step 4: i=2: access arr[2]=15, print 15. Output: 5,10,15.

examples.input: arr=[1,2,3,4], traverse backward and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: len(arr)=4. Step 2: i=3: access arr[3]=4, print 4. Step 3: i=2: access arr[2]=3, print 3. Step 4: i=1: access arr[1]=2, print 2. Step 5: i=0: access arr[0]=1, print 1. Output: 4,3,2,1.

testCases.input: arr=[10,20,30,40], traverse forward from index 1 to 3
arr=[1,2,3,4,5], traverse backward from index 4 to 0

approaches.name: Merge Sort with Counting O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Modify merge sort to count smaller elements during the merge phase. When merging two sorted halves, track how many elements from the right half are smaller than those in the left half. This leverages divide-and-conquer without extra data structures.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Smaller Elements to the Right Using Modified Merge Sort
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of counting smaller elements to the right using merge sort with counting during merge phase.
ladders.explanation: This solution modifies the standard merge sort algorithm to count how many elements to the right are smaller than each element. The key insight is that during the merge phase of two sorted halves, when we take an element from the left half, we can count how many elements from the right half have already been merged (since they're smaller and come from positions to the right). We maintain original indices to track positions and update counts accordingly. The algorithm recursively divides the array, sorts each half while counting, then merges with counting. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to the divide-and-conquer nature of merge sort, and space complexity is O(n) for temporary arrays and recursion stack. This approach avoids extra data structures like BIT by leveraging the natural ordering during merge sort. Common mistakes include forgetting to track original indices, incorrectly updating counts during merge, and not handling duplicate elements properly. The connection to L1 is that this full solution uses the modified merge function as its core subroutine.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Original array with indices: [(5,0), (2,1), (6,2), (1,3)]
Step 2: Divide: Left half [(5,0), (2,1)], Right half [(6,2), (1,3)]
Step 3: Recursively sort left half: Becomes [(2,1), (5,0)] with counts updated
Step 4: Recursively sort right half: Becomes [(1,3), (6,2)] with counts updated
Step 5: Merge with counting: i=0 (left element (2,1)), j=0 (right element (1,3)), since 1<2, take (1,3) from right, increment rightCount=1
Step 6: i=0 (2,1), j=1 (6,2), since 2<6, take (2,1) from left, add rightCount=1 to count[1], result so far: [(1,3), (2,1)]
Step 7: i=1 (5,0), j=1 (6,2), since 5<6, take (5,0) from left, add rightCount=1 to count[0], result: [(1,3), (2,1), (5,0)]
Step 8: Take remaining (6,2) from right, result: [(1,3), (2,1), (5,0), (6,2)]
Step 9: Final counts: For index 0 (value 5): count=2, index 1 (value 2): count=1, index 2 (value 6): count=1, index 3 (value 1): count=0
Step 10: Return [2, 1, 1, 0]

Step 1: Original: [(12,0), (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (0,4), (11,5), (4,6)]
Step 2: Recursively divide and conquer
Step 3: After sorting left half of first division: [(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (12,0)] with counts
Step 4: After sorting right half: [(0,4), (4,6), (11,5)] with counts
Step 5: Merge phase: Initialize rightCount=0
Step 6: Compare (1,1) vs (0,4): 0<1, take (0,4), rightCount=1
Step 7: Compare (1,1) vs (4,6): 1<4, take (1,1), add rightCount=1 to count[1]
Step 8: Compare (2,2) vs (4,6): 2<4, take (2,2), add rightCount=1 to count[2]
Step 9: Compare (3,3) vs (4,6): 3<4, take (3,3), add rightCount=1 to count[3]
Step 10: Compare (12,0) vs (4,6): 4<12, take (4,6), rightCount=2
Step 11: Compare (12,0) vs (11,5): 11<12, take (11,5), rightCount=3
Step 12: Take remaining (12,0), add rightCount=3 to count[0]
Step 13: Final counts calculated throughout recursion: [6, 1, 1, 1, 0, 1, 0]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Modified Merge Function with Counting
ladders.desc: The merge function that combines two sorted halves while counting smaller elements from the right.
ladders.explanation: This is the heart of the solution - a modified merge function that not only merges two sorted arrays but also counts how many elements from the right half are smaller than elements from the left half. The function takes three arrays: left half, right half, and a counts array. It uses two pointers (i for left, j for right) to traverse both halves. The key insight is that when we take an element from the left half, all elements from the right half that have already been merged (j elements) are smaller and come from positions to the right. So we add j to the count for that left element's original index. We must track original indices alongside values. Time complexity is O(n) for merging n elements, and space is O(n) for the merged result. This connects to L0 as it's the core operation called during merge sort recursion. Common mistakes include: not tracking original indices, adding wrong count (should be j not rightCount variable), and not handling when left and right elements are equal (take from left first to maintain stability).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize i=0, j=0, merged=[]
Step 2: Compare left[0]=(2,1) vs right[0]=(1,3): 1<2, take (1,3), j=1, merged=[(1,3)]
Step 3: Compare left[0]=(2,1) vs right[1]=(6,2): 2<6, take (2,1), add j=1 to count[1], i=1, merged=[(1,3), (2,1)]
Step 4: Compare left[1]=(5,0) vs right[1]=(6,2): 5<6, take (5,0), add j=1 to count[0], i=2, merged=[(1,3), (2,1), (5,0)]
Step 5: Take remaining right[1]=(6,2), merged=[(1,3), (2,1), (5,0), (6,2)]
Step 6: Final counts: count[0]=1, count[1]=1, count[2]=0, count[3]=0

Step 1: i=0, j=0, merged=[]
Step 2: Compare (3,0) vs (2,1): 2<3, take (2,1), j=1, merged=[(2,1)]
Step 3: Compare (3,0) vs (5,3): 3<5, take (3,0), add j=1 to count[0], i=1, merged=[(2,1), (3,0)]
Step 4: Compare (7,2) vs (5,3): 5<7, take (5,3), j=2, merged=[(2,1), (3,0), (5,3)]
Step 5: Take remaining (7,2), add j=2 to count[2], merged=[(2,1), (3,0), (5,3), (7,2)]
Step 6: Final counts: count[0]=1, count[1]=0, count[2]=2, count[3]=0

ladders.title: Core Logic — Counting During Merge of Sorted Halves
ladders.desc: The central technique of counting smaller right elements while merging sorted arrays.
ladders.explanation: This is the fundamental insight that makes the solution work: when merging two sorted halves, if we take an element from the left half, all elements from the right half that have already been merged are guaranteed to be smaller (because the halves are sorted) and come from positions to the right (by definition of the right half). Therefore, we can count them as 'smaller elements to the right' for that left element. We maintain a counter of how many elements from the right half have been merged so far. Each time we take an element from the left half, we add this counter to that element's count. The intuition is that merge sort naturally brings smaller elements forward, and by tracking which half elements come from, we get positional information. Time complexity for this counting logic is O(1) per element during merge. This connects to L1 as it's the reasoning behind the modified merge function. Common mistakes include: counting elements from the left half as smaller (they're from the left, not right), not resetting the counter properly, and misunderstanding that we count only when taking from left (not when taking from right).

examples.input: Merging sorted halves: Left=[2,5], Right=[1,6], tracking indices
examples.trace:
Step 1: Both halves sorted: Left=[2,5] (indices 0,1), Right=[1,6] (indices 2,3)
Step 2: Initialize rightCount=0 (elements taken from right so far)
Step 3: Compare 2 (left) vs 1 (right): 1<2, take 1 from right, rightCount=1
Step 4: Compare 2 (left) vs 6 (right): 2<6, take 2 from left, add rightCount=1 to count for element 2
Step 5: Compare 5 (left) vs 6 (right): 5<6, take 5 from left, add rightCount=1 to count for element 5
Step 6: Take remaining 6 from right
Step 7: Result: Element 2 gets +1 count, element 5 gets +1 count

Step 1: Sorted halves: Left=[3,7] (indices 0,2), Right=[2,5] (indices 1,3)
Step 2: rightCount=0
Step 3: Compare 3 vs 2: 2<3, take 2 from right, rightCount=1
Step 4: Compare 3 vs 5: 3<5, take 3 from left, add rightCount=1 to count for element 3
Step 5: Compare 7 vs 5: 5<7, take 5 from right, rightCount=2
Step 6: Take 7 from left, add rightCount=2 to count for element 7
Step 7: Result: Element 3 gets +1, element 7 gets +2

testCases.expected: Counts added: For first 2: +1, For second 2: +1
Counts added: For first 1: +2, For second 1: +2

ladders.title: Building Block — Merge Sort with Index Tracking
ladders.desc: Standard merge sort modified to track original indices alongside values.
ladders.explanation: This is a prerequisite for the counting solution: we need to modify standard merge sort to preserve original indices so we know which count to update for each element. Instead of sorting just values, we sort pairs of (value, original_index). The algorithm works exactly like standard merge sort but operates on these pairs. When comparing elements, we compare values only (not indices). The indices are carried along through the sorting process. This is crucial because after sorting, we need to know each element's original position to update the correct count in the counts array. Time complexity remains O(n log n) since we're just adding constant overhead of storing indices. Space complexity is O(n) for the pairs array. This connects to L2 because once we have indices tracked, we can implement the counting logic during merge. Common mistakes include: comparing indices instead of values during sort, losing index information during recursive calls, and not creating the initial pairs array correctly.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Create pairs: [(5,0), (2,1), (6,2), (1,3)]
Step 2: Divide: Left=[(5,0), (2,1)], Right=[(6,2), (1,3)]
Step 3: Recursively sort left: Becomes [(2,1), (5,0)] (compare values 2<5)
Step 4: Recursively sort right: Becomes [(1,3), (6,2)] (compare values 1<6)
Step 5: Merge: Compare (2,1) vs (1,3): 1<2, take (1,3)
Step 6: Compare (2,1) vs (6,2): 2<6, take (2,1)
Step 7: Compare (5,0) vs (6,2): 5<6, take (5,0)
Step 8: Take remaining (6,2)
Step 9: Result: [(1,3), (2,1), (5,0), (6,2)]

Step 1: Pairs: [(3,0), (1,1), (4,2), (2,3)]
Step 2: Divide: Left=[(3,0), (1,1)], Right=[(4,2), (2,3)]
Step 3: Sort left: Compare 3 vs 1: 1<3, becomes [(1,1), (3,0)]
Step 4: Sort right: Compare 4 vs 2: 2<4, becomes [(2,3), (4,2)]
Step 5: Merge: Compare (1,1) vs (2,3): 1<2, take (1,1)
Step 6: Compare (3,0) vs (2,3): 2<3, take (2,3)
Step 7: Compare (3,0) vs (4,2): 3<4, take (3,0)
Step 8: Take (4,2)
Step 9: Result: [(1,1), (2,3), (3,0), (4,2)]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Standard Merge Sort Algorithm
ladders.desc: The fundamental divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that forms the basis of our solution.
ladders.explanation: Merge sort is a comparison-based sorting algorithm that follows the divide-and-conquer paradigm. It works by recursively dividing the array into two halves until each subarray has one element (which is trivially sorted), then merging the sorted halves back together in sorted order. The merge operation compares elements from both halves and takes the smaller element, progressing through both halves until all elements are merged. This guarantees O(n log n) time complexity because we divide log n times and merge O(n) elements each time. Space complexity is O(n) for the temporary arrays used during merging. This connects to L3 as we need to understand standard merge sort before modifying it with index tracking. Common mistakes include: not handling base case (array of size 0 or 1), incorrect midpoint calculation, not creating temporary arrays correctly, and infinite recursion from incorrect termination conditions.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Array: [5, 2, 6, 1]
Step 2: Divide: Left=[5, 2], Right=[6, 1]
Step 3: Recursively sort left: Divide [5,2] to [5] and [2], merge to [2,5]
Step 4: Recursively sort right: Divide [6,1] to [6] and [1], merge to [1,6]
Step 5: Merge [2,5] and [1,6]: Compare 2 vs 1: 1<2, take 1
Step 6: Compare 2 vs 6: 2<6, take 2
Step 7: Compare 5 vs 6: 5<6, take 5
Step 8: Take remaining 6
Step 9: Result: [1, 2, 5, 6]

Step 1: [3, 1, 4, 2]
Step 2: Divide: Left=[3,1], Right=[4,2]
Step 3: Sort left: Divide [3,1] to [3] and [1], merge to [1,3]
Step 4: Sort right: Divide [4,2] to [4] and [2], merge to [2,4]
Step 5: Merge [1,3] and [2,4]: Compare 1 vs 2: 1<2, take 1
Step 6: Compare 3 vs 2: 2<3, take 2
Step 7: Compare 3 vs 4: 3<4, take 3
Step 8: Take 4
Step 9: Result: [1, 2, 3, 4]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Divide and Conquer with Recursion
ladders.desc: The fundamental programming paradigm of breaking problems into smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Divide and conquer is a fundamental algorithm design paradigm that works by recursively breaking down a problem into two or more sub-problems of the same or related type, until these become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then combined to give a solution to the original problem. Recursion is the natural implementation technique for divide-and-conquer, where a function calls itself with smaller inputs. The key components are: 1) Base case - the simplest possible case that can be solved directly, 2) Divide step - break the problem into smaller subproblems, 3) Conquer step - recursively solve the subproblems, 4) Combine step - merge the solutions. This approach is intuitive because it mimics how we solve complex problems in real life by breaking them into manageable pieces. Time complexity often follows the master theorem. This connects to L4 as merge sort is a classic example of divide-and-conquer. Common beginner mistakes include: missing base case (causing infinite recursion), not making progress toward base case, and incorrect combination of results.

examples.input: Problem: Sum array [1, 2, 3, 4] using divide and conquer
examples.trace:
Step 1: Problem: sum([1,2,3,4])
Step 2: Base case: if array empty, return 0
Step 3: Divide: Split into [1,2] and [3,4]
Step 4: Conquer: Recursively sum([1,2]) and sum([3,4])
Step 5: For sum([1,2]): Divide to [1] and [2], conquer: sum([1])=1, sum([2])=2, combine: 1+2=3
Step 6: For sum([3,4]): Divide to [3] and [4], conquer: sum([3])=3, sum([4])=4, combine: 3+4=7
Step 7: Combine: 3+7=10
Step 8: Result: 10

examples.input: Problem: Find maximum in [5, 2, 8, 1] using divide and conquer
examples.trace:
Step 1: Problem: max([5,2,8,1])
Step 2: Base case: if single element, return it
Step 3: Divide: Split into [5,2] and [8,1]
Step 4: Conquer: max([5,2]) and max([8,1])
Step 5: For max([5,2]): Divide to [5] and [2], conquer: max([5])=5, max([2])=2, combine: max(5,2)=5
Step 6: For max([8,1]): Divide to [8] and [1], conquer: max([8])=8, max([1])=1, combine: max(8,1)=8
Step 7: Combine: max(5,8)=8
Step 8: Result: 8

approaches.name: Segment Tree O(n log m)
approaches.summary: Similar to BIT but using a segment tree to query and update frequency counts of values. Process from right to left, querying for smaller values and updating the tree. Also requires coordinate compression for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Smaller Elements to the Right Using Segment Tree
ladders.desc: Complete solution using segment tree with coordinate compression to count elements smaller to the right.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires counting for each element in an array how many elements to its right are smaller. The segment tree approach solves this efficiently by processing the array from right to left. First, we perform coordinate compression to map all unique values to indices in a compressed range [0, m-1], where m is the number of unique values. This reduces the segment tree size to O(m) instead of O(max_value). We then build a segment tree that supports range sum queries and point updates. The segment tree stores frequency counts of values encountered so far. Starting from the rightmost element, for each element, we query the segment tree for the sum of frequencies of values in the range [0, compressed_index-1], which gives the count of smaller elements seen so far (i.e., to the right). We then update the segment tree at the compressed index by incrementing its frequency. This process is repeated for all elements, building the result array. The intuition is that by processing from right to left, we maintain a dynamic frequency count of elements we've already processed (which are to the right of the current element), and the segment tree allows efficient querying and updating. Time complexity is O(n log m) for n elements and m unique values, due to O(log m) per query/update. Space complexity is O(m) for the segment tree and compression maps. Connection to L1: This uses the segment tree query and update operations from L1 as subroutines. Common mistakes include forgetting coordinate compression for large value ranges, incorrect query ranges (off-by-one errors), and not handling duplicate values properly in compression. Real code pattern: def countSmaller(nums): compress mapping; build segtree; result = []; for i from n-1 to 0: idx = compress[nums[i]]; result.append(query(0, idx-1)); update(idx, 1); reverse result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Coordinate compression. Unique sorted values: [1,2,5,6] → mapping: {1:0, 2:1, 5:2, 6:3}, m=4. Step 2: Build segment tree of size 4, initialized to zeros. Step 3: Process from right to left. i=3, nums[3]=1, idx=0. Query range [0,-1] (empty) → count=0. Update idx=0 to 1. Tree: [1,0,0,0]. Result so far: [0]. i=2, nums[2]=6, idx=3. Query range [0,2] sum=1 (from idx 0) → count=1. Update idx=3 to 1. Tree: [1,0,0,1]. Result: [1,0]. i=1, nums[1]=2, idx=1. Query range [0,0] sum=1 → count=1. Update idx=1 to 1. Tree: [1,1,0,1]. Result: [1,1,0]. i=0, nums[0]=5, idx=2. Query range [0,1] sum=2 (from idx 0 and 1) → count=2. Update idx=2 to 1. Tree: [1,1,1,1]. Result: [2,1,1,0]. Reverse not needed as built from right. Final: [2,1,1,0].

Step 1: Coordinate compression. Unique values: [1] → mapping: {1:0}, m=1. Step 2: Build segment tree of size 1, initialized to zero. Step 3: Process from right to left. i=2, nums[2]=1, idx=0. Query range [0,-1] → count=0. Update idx=0 to 1. Tree: [1]. Result: [0]. i=1, nums[1]=1, idx=0. Query range [0,-1] → count=0. Update idx=0 to 2. Tree: [2]. Result: [0,0]. i=0, nums[0]=1, idx=0. Query range [0,-1] → count=0. Update idx=0 to 3. Tree: [3]. Result: [0,0,0]. Final: [0,0,0].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Segment Tree Query and Update for Frequency Counts
ladders.desc: Implement segment tree operations to query sum of a range and update a point.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine involves building and using a segment tree that supports two operations: query(l, r) to get the sum of elements in the range [l, r], and update(idx, val) to add val to the element at index idx. In the context of counting smaller elements, the segment tree stores frequency counts of compressed values. The tree is built as an array-based segment tree where each node represents the sum of frequencies in a segment. For query(l, r), we recursively traverse the tree, combining results from overlapping segments. For update(idx, val), we update the leaf node and propagate changes up to the root. The intuition is that the segment tree allows O(log m) time for both operations, making it efficient for dynamic frequency tracking. Time complexity per operation is O(log m) where m is the number of leaves (compressed range size). Space complexity is O(m) for the tree array. Connection to L0: This is used in the main algorithm to query counts of smaller values and update frequencies. Common mistakes include incorrect tree size (should be 4*m for safety), off-by-one in range queries, and not using lazy propagation (not needed here as updates are point updates). Real code pattern: class SegmentTree: def __init__(size): tree = [0]*4*size; def query(node, start, end, l, r): if r < start or end < l: return 0; if l <= start and end <= r: return tree[node]; mid = (start+end)//2; return query(2*node, start, mid, l, r) + query(2*node+1, mid+1, end, l, r); def update(node, start, end, idx, val): if start == end: tree[node] += val; else: mid = (start+end)//2; if idx <= mid: update(2*node, start, mid, idx, val); else: update(2*node+1, mid+1, end, idx, val); tree[node] = tree[2*node] + tree[2*node+1].

examples.input: Tree initially zeros, size=4. Operations: update(0,1), query(0,2), update(2,1), query(0,3)
examples.output: After update(0,1): tree reflects freq at idx 0=1. Query(0,2) returns 1. After update(2,1): tree adds freq at idx 2=1. Query(0,3) returns 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Build tree of size 4: tree = [0,0,0,0,...]. Step 2: update(0,1). Start at node 1, range [0,3]. idx=0 in left child. Recursively update to leaf at idx 0, set to 1. Propagate up: parent nodes update sums. Tree becomes: root sum=1. Step 3: query(0,2). Start at node 1, range [0,3]. Query range [0,2] overlaps. Check children: left child [0,1] fully in range? No, partially. Recursively: left child query [0,1] vs [0,2] → left leaf 0 gives 1, right leaf 1 gives 0, sum=1. Right child [2,3] partially in range: leaf 2 gives 0 (not updated yet), leaf 3 out of range. Total sum=1. Step 4: update(2,1). Update leaf at idx 2 to 1. Propagate up: root sum becomes 2. Step 5: query(0,3). Full range query returns root sum=2.

examples.input: Tree size=2, initial zeros. Operations: update(1,2), query(0,1), update(0,1), query(0,1)
examples.output: After update(1,2): tree has 2 at idx 1. Query(0,1) returns 2. After update(0,1): tree adds 1 at idx 0. Query(0,1) returns 3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Build tree size 2: tree = [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: update(1,2). Update leaf idx 1 to 2. Propagate: root sum=2. Step 3: query(0,1). Full range query returns root sum=2. Step 4: update(0,1). Update leaf idx 0 to 1. Propagate: root sum=3. Step 5: query(0,1). Returns 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Process Array from Right to Left with Dynamic Counting
ladders.desc: The central technique of iterating backwards and maintaining counts of seen elements.
ladders.explanation: The core logic involves processing the input array from the last element to the first, while dynamically maintaining a data structure (like a frequency array or segment tree) that counts how many times each value has been seen so far. For each element at position i, we query this data structure to find how many values smaller than nums[i] have been encountered (which are elements to the right, since we're moving right to left). This count is stored as the result for position i. Then, we update the data structure to include nums[i] in the counts. The intuition is that by processing from right to left, we ensure that when we query for smaller elements, we only consider elements that are to the right of the current position, because those are the ones we've already processed and added to our data structure. This avoids the need for nested loops and reduces time complexity from O(n²) to O(n log n) with efficient data structures. Time complexity depends on the data structure used; with a simple array it could be O(n²), but with segment tree it's O(n log m). Space complexity is O(m) for the frequency storage. Connection to L1: This logic uses the segment tree from L1 as the data structure for efficient queries and updates. Common mistakes include processing from left to right (which counts elements to the left), not updating the data structure after querying, and incorrect handling of equal elements (should count only smaller, not equal). Real code pattern: result = []; freq_structure = init(); for i from n-1 down to 0: count = query_freq_structure_for_values_smaller_than(nums[i]); result.append(count); update_freq_structure(nums[i]); reverse result if needed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize freq array of size 4 with zeros: freq = [0,0,0,0]. Step 2: Process from right to left. i=2, nums[2]=2. Query for values smaller than 2: sum of freq[0] and freq[1] = 0+0=0. Result: [0]. Update freq[2] += 1 → freq = [0,0,1,0]. i=1, nums[1]=1. Query for values smaller than 1: sum freq[0] = 0. Result: [0,0]. Update freq[1] += 1 → freq = [0,1,1,0]. i=0, nums[0]=3. Query for values smaller than 3: sum freq[0]+freq[1]+freq[2] = 0+1+1=2? Wait, careful: smaller than 3 means values 0,1,2. Sum = 0+1+1=2. But expected output is [1,0,0]? Let's recalc: Actually, for [3,1,2], smaller to right: for 3, elements to right are [1,2], both smaller → count=2; for 1, elements to right [2], 2 is not smaller → count=0; for 2, no elements to right → count=0. So output should be [2,0,0]. My trace error: At i=0, nums[0]=3, query for smaller than 3: freq[0]=0, freq[1]=1, freq[2]=1, sum=2. Yes, result [2,0,0]. Update freq[3] +=1. So correct trace: Start freq=[0,0,0,0]. i=2: nums[2]=2, query <2: sum freq[0]+freq[1]=0, result [0], update freq[2]=1. i=1: nums[1]=1, query <1: sum freq[0]=0, result [0,0], update freq[1]=1. i=0: nums[0]=3, query <3: sum freq[0]+freq[1]+freq[2]=0+1+1=2, result [2,0,0], update freq[3]=1. Final result [2,0,0].

Step 1: freq array size 4 (values 1-3 mapped to 1-3, but let's use 0-3 for simplicity, assume values 1,2,3). Actually, better to map: values 1,2,3. Use array indices 1,2,3. Initialize freq[1]=freq[2]=freq[3]=0. Step 2: Process right to left. i=2, nums[2]=3. Query for values smaller than 3: sum freq[1]+freq[2]=0. Result [0]. Update freq[3]=1. i=1, nums[1]=2. Query for values smaller than 2: sum freq[1]=0. Result [0,0]. Update freq[2]=1. i=0, nums[0]=1. Query for values smaller than 1: sum freq[<1]=0. Result [0,0,0]. Update freq[1]=1. Final [0,0,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Coordinate Compression for Efficient Value Mapping
ladders.desc: Map unique values to compressed indices to reduce segment tree size.
ladders.explanation: Coordinate compression is a technique used to map a set of values (which may be large or non-contiguous) to a contiguous range of indices starting from 0. This is crucial for segment tree approaches where the tree size depends on the range of values. For the count smaller problem, the input array may contain large integers or floating points, but we only care about their relative order. By sorting the unique values and assigning each a rank (compressed index), we create a mapping from original value to index in [0, m-1], where m is the number of unique values. This reduces the segment tree size from O(max_value) to O(m), making it efficient. The intuition is that we preserve the order of values (if a < b, then compressed index of a < compressed index of b), so queries for smaller values translate to queries for smaller compressed indices. Steps: 1. Collect all unique values from the input array. 2. Sort the unique values. 3. Create a mapping (e.g., dictionary) from each value to its index in the sorted list. 4. Optionally, create a reverse mapping. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting, where n is the number of elements. Space complexity is O(m) for the mapping. Connection to L2: This compressed mapping is used in the core logic to convert original values to indices for segment tree operations. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (should map same values to same index), using unstable sorting, and forgetting to update the mapping when values change. Real code pattern: def compress(nums): unique = sorted(set(nums)); mapping = {val: i for i, val in enumerate(unique)}; return mapping, len(unique).

examples.trace: Step 1: Collect unique values: set = {5,2,6,1}. Step 2: Sort unique values: [1,2,5,6]. Step 3: Create mapping: index 0 → 1, so mapping[1]=0; index 1 → 2, mapping[2]=1; index 2 → 5, mapping[5]=2; index 3 → 6, mapping[6]=3. Step 4: m = length of sorted list = 4.
Step 1: Collect unique values: set = {10,20,30}. Step 2: Sort: [10,20,30]. Step 3: Mapping: index 0 → 10, mapping[10]=0; index 1 → 20, mapping[20]=1; index 2 → 30, mapping[30]=2. Step 4: m=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Range Sum Query on an Array
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of elements in a given subarray range.
ladders.explanation: A range sum query involves calculating the sum of all elements in an array between two indices, inclusive. For example, given an array arr and indices l and r, the sum is arr[l] + arr[l+1] + ... + arr[r]. This is a fundamental operation used in many algorithms, including as a building block for segment trees. The naive approach is to iterate from l to r, summing elements, which takes O(r-l+1) time. For frequent queries, this can be inefficient, leading to O(n²) time in worst case. More efficient methods include prefix sums, which precompute cumulative sums so that range sum can be computed in O(1) time as prefix[r] - prefix[l-1] (with care for l=0). However, in dynamic scenarios where array values update, segment trees are used for O(log n) queries. The intuition is that breaking the range into smaller segments allows faster computation. Time complexity for naive is O(k) where k is range length; for prefix sums, O(1) after O(n) preprocessing; for segment trees, O(log n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the array or additional structures. Connection to L3: In coordinate compression, we might use range sums to query frequencies, but in L3, we use mapping instead. Actually, connection to L2: range sum queries are used in the core logic to count frequencies of smaller values. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices, not handling empty ranges (l > r), and integer overflow for large sums. Real code pattern: def range_sum_naive(arr, l, r): sum=0; for i in range(l, r+1): sum += arr[i]; return sum. Or with prefix: prefix[0]=0; for i in range(n): prefix[i+1]=prefix[i]+arr[i]; def range_sum_prefix(l,r): return prefix[r+1]-prefix[l].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array indices 0-based: arr[0]=1, arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3, arr[3]=4, arr[4]=5. Step 2: Range l=1 to r=3 inclusive: elements arr[1]=2, arr[2]=3, arr[3]=4. Step 3: Sum = 2+3+4 = 9.
Step 1: arr[0]=10, arr[1]=20, arr[2]=30. Step 2: Range l=0 to r=2: all elements. Step 3: Sum = 10+20+30 = 60.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Understand arrays, indexing, and looping through elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index or key. In programming, arrays are typically zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. Basic operations include accessing an element by its index (e.g., arr[i]), updating an element (e.g., arr[i] = value), and iterating through all elements using loops. Iteration can be done forward (from first to last) or backward (from last to first). For example, to traverse an array of size n, we can use a for loop: for i in range(n): process arr[i]. To traverse backward: for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): process arr[i]. This foundation is essential for manipulating data in algorithms. The intuition is that arrays provide random access, allowing quick lookups by index, but insertion/deletion in the middle can be costly. Time complexity for access is O(1), for iteration O(n). Space complexity is O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: Range sum queries rely on accessing array elements by index and iterating over ranges. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing arr[n] when valid indices are 0 to n-1), off-by-one errors in loops, and confusing zero-indexing with one-indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [1,2,3]; n = len(arr); for i in range(n): print(arr[i]); for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): print(arr[i]).

examples.input: Array [5,10,15], iterate forward and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr = [5,10,15], indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. Step 2: Forward iteration: i=0, print arr[0]=5; i=1, print arr[1]=10; i=2, print arr[2]=15. Output: 5,10,15.

examples.input: Array [3,6,9], iterate backward and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: arr = [3,6,9], indices 0→3, 1→6, 2→9. Step 2: Backward iteration: start i=2, print arr[2]=9; i=1, print arr[1]=6; i=0, print arr[0]=3. Output: 9,6,3.

testCases.input: arr=[4,5,6], update index 1 to 10, then access index 1
arr=[7,8,9], iterate forward and sum elements
arr=[2,4,6], iterate backward and list elements
arr=[3,1,4], iterate forward and find max
arr=[5,2,8], iterate backward and print each
